2025-12-26.thesis: D-Wave major company event scheduled January 27, 2026
2025-12-26.exit_case: QBTS surged ~20% in one session; January 27 event is now widely publicized, informational edge may already be priced in.
2025-12-26.assessment: First week of tracking. Catalyst is intact: D-Wave's major company event is confirmed for January 27, 2026 (per Yahoo Finance Dec 26), still ~32 days out. QBTS ripped ~20% on Dec 23 amid a sector-wide quantum computing wave (Google Willow, IBM, Microsoft hype cycle), not specifically tied to the Jan 27 reveal. The event content remains unknown — product launch, commercial contract, partnership, or technology milestone are all plausible. That uncertainty is where residual edge lives. Key risk: the move from ~$27 to ~$32 in one session front-runs some of the catalyst premium, so the reward/risk window has compressed. Still, the specific event has not occurred and no details have leaked. Monitor for any pre-announcement leaks or investor-day agenda releases that would accelerate the 'priced in' dynamic. QBTS remains the only pure-play vehicle.
2025-12-26.exit_advice: Hold into January 27 event; exit on the day-of or immediately after announcement regardless of content — classic buy-the-rumor, sell-the-news setup.

2026-01-02.thesis: D-Wave major company event scheduled January 27, 2026
2026-01-02.exit_case: Cumulative ~40% surge since entry (Dec 23 +20%, Dec 31 +17%) has largely priced Jan 27 premium already.
2026-01-02.assessment: The Jan 27, 2026 D-Wave major company event remains pending and unresolved — catalyst_resolved stays false. However, a new near-term event has surfaced: QBTS is presenting at CES 2026 (Jan 7-10), which drove another ~17% surge around Dec 31 on top of the ~20% Dec 23 move. Total cumulative gain from entry is now likely ~40%+, severely compressing the remaining risk/reward window for the Jan 27 catalyst. The key risks identified at entry (front-running, pre-announcement leaks) are now materially elevated. Critical watch items: (1) does the CES presentation pre-reveal the Jan 27 event content, collapsing the remaining edge? (2) does the quantum sector hype cycle fade post-CES before Jan 27, causing a sell-the-CES-news flush? The Fool article noting institutional preference for larger quantum plays over pure-plays like QBTS is a mild headwind. The thesis is technically intact but the risk/reward is now marginal — the original edge was the gap between the stock price and the unpriced Jan 27 event; that gap has narrowed dramatically. QBTS remains the only pure-play vehicle.
2026-01-02.exit_advice: Hold through Jan 27 event, but treat CES 2026 presentation (Jan 7-10) as an interim tripwire: if D-Wave reveals substantive content there that overlaps with the Jan 27 event, reassess immediately as a pre-leak. Otherwise, watch for post-CES profit-taking as a potential secondary entry degradation signal.

2026-01-09.thesis: D-Wave major company event scheduled January 27, 2026
2026-01-09.exit_case: Quantum sector bubble burst (Rigetti -54%, mainstream bubble warnings); ~40% pre-run likely reversed by sector-wide crash before Jan 27 even arrives.
2026-01-09.assessment: The Jan 27 D-Wave event is still pending (catalyst_resolved=false), but the thesis is no longer live. Entry premise: capture the gap between market price and an unpriced company-event premium. That gap closed in two waves — Dec 23 (~20%) and Dec 31 (~17%) — for a ~40%+ cumulative move. CES (Jan 7-10) passed without pre-revealing Jan 27 content, but post-CES the sector flushed hard: Rigetti is -54% from recent highs, and Motley Fool published multiple pieces (Jan 8) explicitly naming D-Wave/QBTS as bubble candidates set to crash in 2026. The quantum hype cycle that created the pre-run premium is now working in reverse, and QBTS almost certainly gave back a large portion of those gains this week. Even if the Jan 27 event delivers a positive surprise, it must first overcome a deeply negative sector re-rating — a setup that inverts rather than confirms the original risk/reward. Thesis is broken; exit.
2026-01-09.exit_advice: Exit QBTS. The original edge was the gap between stock price and an unpriced Jan 27 event; that gap closed via a ~40% pre-run. Now the sector is in a full unwind (Rigetti -54%, multiple Motley Fool bubble-burst pieces naming D-Wave specifically). Holding 18 more days in a broken-sector environment for a catalyst whose premium has already been built then likely erased is not defensible.

No residual informational edge from this catalyst remains.

2026-03-13.thesis: Strait of Hormuz chokepoint risk spikes oil-tanker day-rates
2026-03-13.exit_case: A rapid Iran-US diplomatic breakthrough could collapse Hormuz risk premium and day-rates overnight.
2026-03-13.assessment: First week. Hormuz chokepoint risk remains live and is actively lifting VLCC day-rates per this week's news. BWET is described as still niche and under-owned — no crowding signal yet, suggesting the freight-futures curve has not fully priced the risk spike. Catalyst is unambiguously still pending. Key watch items: any Iran-US back-channel talks, IRGC naval activity, or US carrier-group posture changes. BWET is the sole known vehicle and the cleanest pure-play (freight-futures ETN, not shipping equity), so no vehicle rotation needed at this stage. Hold and monitor diplomatic headlines as the primary exit trigger.
2026-03-13.exit_advice: Exit if a credible Iran-US deal or Hormuz de-escalation is announced — that resolves the specific catalyst regardless of where BWET trades at that moment.

2026-03-20.thesis: Strait of Hormuz chokepoint risk spikes oil-tanker day-rates
2026-03-20.exit_case: Week's only Hormuz mention is a passive 'investors watch' note — no fresh escalation driving day-rates higher.
2026-03-20.assessment: Second week; the chokepoint risk is unresolved and still being cited by market participants (FTSE energy-stock move linked explicitly to Hormuz watching). No ceasefire, back-channel deal, or carrier-group posture change has been reported. The news flow is thin — one brief secondary mention — which is neither a resolution signal nor a new escalation catalyst. BWET remains under-owned relative to equity tanker names and is the cleanest pure-play on freight-futures rather than shipping-company P&L. Devil's advocate is noted: absent a fresh flashpoint this week, day-rate momentum may stall. But stalling is not reversal and the structural threat (Iran leverage over the strait) is intact. Hold BWET; primary watch remains any formal Iran-US diplomatic announcement or IRGC naval stand-down order.
2026-03-20.exit_advice: Exit if Iran-US diplomatic channel reopens formally, IRGC naval activity stands down, or VLCC day-rate indices show sustained multi-week decline without new threat event.

2026-03-27.thesis: Strait of Hormuz chokepoint risk spikes oil-tanker day-rates
2026-03-27.exit_case: Three weeks in, zero fresh Hormuz incidents; the day-rate spike may already be decaying without any formal resolution.
2026-03-27.assessment: No news this week is the core problem. The devil's advocate case is genuinely strong: the catalyst was a *spike* in day-rates driven by acute Hormuz risk, and three weeks of no new incident or escalation suggests the risk premium embedded in freight futures is likely eroding even without a formal resolution. The structural threat (Iran's leverage) is unchanged, but structural threats that stop generating headlines stop generating freight-rate moves. BWET as a freight-futures ETN decays on carry if the curve flattens — unlike an equity tanker name, it has no operational upside cushion. Thesis is technically still live (no ceasefire, no deal, no IRGC stand-down), but conviction is dropping. Hold BWET narrowly; any further news vacuum next week should flip this to exit.
2026-03-27.exit_advice: Exit if confirmed Iran-US back-channel deal, IRGC naval stand-down, or a sustained multi-week collapse in VLCC day-rates signals the risk premium has fully drained. The silence this week is the loudest warning yet — a fourth consecutive week of no new flashpoint should prompt a hard re-evaluation of whether the spike thesis has quietly expired.

2026-04-03.thesis: Strait of Hormuz chokepoint risk spikes oil-tanker day-rates
2026-04-03.exit_case: Trump signals willingness to end war without reopening Hormuz — de-escalation removes the acute risk premium that drove the day-rate spike.
2026-04-03.assessment: The entire thesis rested on ACUTE Hormuz risk spiking day-rates — a fear-premium trade, not a physical-disruption trade. Three weeks of news vacuum had already eroded conviction, and last week's note explicitly said the next news vacuum should flip this to exit. Instead we got something worse than silence: a direct presidential de-escalation signal. 'Willing to end war without reopening Hormuz' tells us (1) there is active back-channel movement toward war termination, and (2) even the US is no longer anchoring settlement terms to Hormuz re-opening. The tail-risk premium that drove the day-rate spike is now being priced out. The physical restriction of the strait may persist — but a permanently restricted strait at stable low-risk is a structural ton-miles thesis, not the risk-spike thesis entered on. The catalyst (acute risk spike) has peaked and is reversing. Exiting.
2026-04-03.exit_advice: Exit BWET immediately. The presidential de-escalation signal is the clean exit trigger that last week's journal explicitly called for. BWET is a freight-futures ETN with no operational cushion — carry erosion accelerates as the risk curve flattens. Do not re-enter on a 'Hormuz stays closed' structural thesis; that is a different trade requiring a different vehicle.

2026-06-19.thesis: $85B UNP–Norfolk Southern merger awaiting STB regulatory approval
2026-06-19.exit_case: STB Class I railroad merger reviews run 3+ years; no binary ruling imminent, so near-term edge is structurally thin.
2026-06-19.assessment: First week of coverage. The catalyst — STB regulatory approval for the $85B UNP–NSC merger — is firmly pending. This week's news confirms UNP is making formal supporting submissions to the STB, which is early-stage procedural activity, not a resolution signal. STB reviews of Class I railroad combinations under modern rules are notoriously long (historically 3+ years), so no ruling is imminent. The thesis is alive but requires patience. Key milestones to track: STB acceptance of the application, formal hearing schedule, DOJ Antitrust Division posture, shipper/competitor opposition filings (CN, BNSF, CSX likely to intervene), and any political pressure from agricultural or labor constituencies. UNP is an impure vehicle — as acquirer it bears integration risk and regulatory uncertainty, unlike the target (NSC) which would trade a cleaner spread to deal price. NSC is not in the known vehicle set, so UNP remains the designated vehicle; consider flagging NSC for addition if spread-to-close becomes the primary signal. No material new information this week beyond the filing confirmation.
2026-06-19.exit_advice: Exit on STB approval or denial (catalyst resolved either way); also exit on merger withdrawal, abandonment, or DOJ block that ends the deal.

2026-06-26.thesis: $85B UNP–Norfolk Southern merger awaiting STB regulatory approval
2026-06-26.exit_case: Zero news this week; STB Class I reviews historically run 3+ years — thesis has no near-term resolution and suffers pure time-decay.
2026-06-26.assessment: Second week of coverage; no material developments. The STB regulatory process remains at the very earliest procedural stage — formal submission support, no acceptance or docketing announced. The 3+ year historical review window for Class I combinations means no ruling is imminent. The exit case this week is legitimate: this is pure time-decay with no catalyst progression. The thesis is technically alive (approval still pending, not denied or withdrawn), but the holding cost is rising without offsetting information flow. UNP as acquirer remains an impure vehicle — it absorbs integration risk and regulatory drag rather than trading a clean spread to deal close. If NSC (the target) were added to the known vehicle set, it would be the cleaner express of this thesis. No change to vehicle or stance; monitor STB docket for application acceptance.
2026-06-26.exit_advice: Hold but flag: the time-decay argument is real. With no news flow and a multi-year STB timeline, UNP offers little event-driven edge in the near term. The position is not wrong, but it is idle — watch for STB application acceptance, DOJ posture announcement, or intervenor filings as the next live milestones.

2026-07-03.thesis: $85B UNP–Norfolk Southern merger awaiting STB regulatory approval
2026-07-03.exit_case: Three consecutive weeks of zero news; STB approval is 3+ years away — pure time-decay, no near-term catalyst progression in an impure acquirer vehicle.
2026-07-03.assessment: Third consecutive week with no material news. The STB process remains at the absolute earliest procedural stage — no formal application acceptance, no docketing, no hearing schedule, no DOJ posture signal. The specific catalyst (STB approval) is unambiguously still pending and has not been granted or denied, so catalyst_resolved stays false and thesis_live stays true — but this is a minimal, technical 'alive.' The structural problems identified in weeks 1 and 2 are compounding: (1) information velocity is zero and the review horizon is 3+ years, making this a pure carry trade with no near-term binary; (2) UNP as acquirer is the least efficient vehicle — it absorbs integration execution risk and regulatory overhang rather than expressing a clean deal-close spread; (3) with no news flow, there is no edge advantage over a passive holder. The next meaningful milestone remains STB docket acceptance of the formal application — that event would restart genuine catalyst monitoring. Until then, the position is costing time and opportunity. NSC (target) remains the structurally cleaner vehicle but is outside the known set.
2026-07-03.exit_advice: Thesis technically alive but degrading rapidly: zero information velocity for three straight weeks, 3+ year review horizon, and UNP absorbs acquirer drag rather than trading a clean spread. Seriously consider exiting to redeploy capital until a concrete STB milestone (application acceptance, docketing, hearing schedule) materializes.
